dublinCore.dc:title: 119 HR 9676 IH: Examining Opioid Treatment Infrastructure Act of 2026
dublinCore.dc:rights: Pursuant to Title 17 Section 105 of the United States Code, this file is not subject to copyright protection and is in the public domain.

action.action-desc: Mr. Foster introduced the following bill; which was referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Natural Resources, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provisions as fall within the jurisdiction of the committee concerned

form.official-title: To direct the Comptroller General of the United States to evaluate and report on the inpatient and outpatient treatment capacity, availability, and needs of the United States with respect to opioid-use disorders, and for other purposes.

section.text: This Act may be cited as the Examining Opioid Treatment Infrastructure Act of 2026.

section.header: Study on treatment infrastructure for opioid-use disorders
section.text: Not later than 24 months after the date of enactment of this Act, the Comptroller General of the United States shall initiate an evaluation of, and submit to Congress a report on, the inpatient and outpatient treatment capacity, availability, and needs of the United States with respect to opioid-use disorders, including, to the extent data are available—

paragraph.text: the capacity of acute residential or inpatient detoxification programs;
the capacity of inpatient clinical stabilization programs, transitional residential support services, and residential rehabilitation programs;
the capacity of demographic specific residential or inpatient treatment programs, such as those designed for pregnant women or adolescents;
geographical differences in the availability of residential and outpatient treatment and recovery options across the continuum of care;

the availability of residential and outpatient treatment programs that offer treatment options based on reliable scientific evidence of efficacy for the treatment of substance-use disorders, including the use of Food and Drug Administration-approved medicines and evidence-based nonpharmacological therapies;
the number of patients in residential and specialty outpatient treatment services;
an assessment of the need for residential and outpatient treatment across the continuum of care;

the availability of residential and outpatient treatment programs to American Indians and Alaska Natives through an Indian health program (as defined by section 4 of the Indian Health Care Improvement Act (25 U.S.C. 1603)); and
the barriers (including technological barriers) at the Federal, State, and local levels to real-time reporting of de-identified information on drug overdoses and ways to overcome such barriers.
